# The gut microbiome

> *Meet the trillions of microbes living in your gut. The first-year goal is **recognition**:
> know who is in there and how many, understand the one job they do that you cannot — turning
> fibre into fuel — and learn to tell the real, diet-modifiable science from the hype, which on
> this topic is thick. Not species lists, not prescribing — the ecosystem and its evidence.*

## Meet your microbiome {#sec-overview}

Your last chapters followed food into the body and tracked what your own cells do with it. But
your own cells are not the only ones eating. The large intestine houses a dense **community of
microbes** — mostly bacteria, plus some archaea, fungi and viruses — that lives on what your small
intestine could not digest and, in return, does chemistry your body cannot. This chapter answers
four linked questions: **who is in there, what do they do for you, what feeds them, and what does
it mean for a patient?**

Two habits of mind run through it. The first is **ecosystem thinking**: this is a community, shaped
by what you feed it, best understood the way an ecologist understands a habitat — not a single
organ to fix with a pill. The second is **honesty about hype.** The microbiome is a genuine,
diet-modifiable system *and* the single most over-marketed topic in consumer nutrition. Half of what
follows is learning the real mechanism; the other half is learning to watch the marketing claims
collapse the moment you ask "by what mechanism?"

---

## Who lives there, and how many {#sec-composition}

**It is a community, and bacteria carry the story.** The gut holds trillions of microbes:
**bacteria** dominate by mass and function, alongside archaea, fungi (the "mycobiome") and viruses
(mostly bacteria-infecting phages) that are minor players at this level [@matijasic2020beyond]. And
it lives **mostly in the colon.** Microbial density climbs steeply along the tract — sparse in the
acidic stomach and brisk small intestine, then densest by orders of magnitude in the **colon**,
whose slow transit, near-neutral pH and minimal absorption (the anatomy is Chapter 5's) make it the
body's **fermentation chamber** [@donaldson2016gutbiogeography].

::: {.callout-warning}
## The 10:1 myth — and the 1–2 kg myth
You will still read that microbes outnumber your own cells **10 to 1**. They do not. A careful 2016
recount put the ratio at roughly **1:1** — about 38 trillion bacteria to 30 trillion human cells
(most of which are small red blood cells, ~84% of the total, which the old estimate ignored)
[@sender2016revised]. The same recount puts the total bacterial mass at about **0.2 kg**, not the
"1–2 kg" often quoted (that figure conflates bacteria with the whole weight of colon contents).
Carry the *ratio* and the *mass*; the exact counts are trivia.
:::

**The real "wow" is genes, not numbers.** The collective microbial gene catalogue — the
**metagenome** — is on the order of **150 times** the ~20,000 genes in your own genome
[@qin2010catalogue]. That vast "second genome" is what lets these microbes extract energy and make
compounds your own cells cannot — the theme of this whole chapter.

**Who is there — with current names.** Two bacterial phyla dominate the healthy adult gut:
**Bacillota** (you may see the old name *Firmicutes*) and **Bacteroidota** (formerly
*Bacteroidetes*), with smaller contributions from **Actinomycetota** (which includes
*Bifidobacterium*) and others [@oren2021valid]. You do not need the genus lists; you need the idea
that a few big groups dominate and the names were formally updated in 2021.

**Different people, similar jobs.** Here is the surprise that shapes everything: two perfectly
healthy people can carry very **different species** yet perform the same **functions** — fibre
fermentation, vitamin synthesis, SCFA production are each done by many different microbes. We share
a **functional core, not a taxonomic core** — a property called **functional redundancy**
[@moya2016functional]. (An older idea that everyone belongs to one of "three enterotypes" is now
read as a **continuous gradient**, not fixed categories [@knights2014rethinking].) This is also why
**diversity** is only a *loose* health marker: broadly, a richer community tends to be a more
resilient one, but there are clear exceptions, so treat diversity as **one context-dependent signal
— not a number to maximise or buy in a bottle** [@mcburney2019healthy].

## How your microbiome grows up {#sec-development}

Your microbiome is not fixed at birth; it **assembles over the first years of life** and keeps
shifting after. A newborn's gut is sparsely and erratically colonised; the community is shaped first
by **delivery mode** and then, more durably, by **feeding** — breast milk carries special sugars
(**human milk oligosaccharides**, HMOs) that the baby cannot digest but that selectively feed
*Bifidobacterium*. Weaning onto solids diversifies it, and it becomes **adult-like by about age
three** (a gradual approach, not a switch). Late in life, in frailty and especially
institutional care, **diversity tends to decline** [@backhed2015dynamics; @claesson2012elderly].

::: {.callout-note}
## A C-section does not "permanently damage" the microbiome
Babies born by caesarean start with a *different* early community, but the difference is largely
**transient** — feeding mode is the stronger, more durable driver, and the microbiome converges over
the first years. "Vaginal seeding" (swabbing a C-section baby with vaginal fluid) is **not** an
evidence-based fix and is advised against outside research, partly for infection risk
[@acog2017vaginalseeding]. A useful antidote to the guilt this topic often provokes.
:::

---

## The main event: fibre becomes fuel {#sec-scfa}

This is the chapter's centrepiece and the microbiome's single most important job for you. Recall
from Chapter 5 that your own enzymes cannot break down **dietary fibre** and **resistant starch** —
so they travel, undigested, all the way to the colon. There, the anaerobic bacteria do what your
enzymes could not: they **ferment** it. The distinction is everything — *digestion* is your enzymes
hydrolysing food in the small intestine; **fermentation** is bacterial metabolism, without oxygen,
in the colon [@koh2016scfa].

The pay-off is a family of small molecules — the **short-chain fatty acids (SCFAs)** — that fuel
your gut and signal to your body. Step through how it works below: what arrives, who ferments it,
what comes out, and where each product goes.

<div data-island="scfa-flow" data-src="/diagrams/scfa-flow.yml"></div>

**Three short-chain fatty acids, each with a job.** Fermentation produces mainly three SCFAs, in an
approximate ratio of **60:20:20** (it varies with diet, substrate and site — treat it as "roughly
60:20:20") [@cummings1987scfa]:

- **Acetate** (the most abundant) is poorly extracted by the liver and travels **systemically** to
  peripheral tissues and other bacteria.
- **Propionate** is taken up by the **liver** through the portal vein, where it is a
  **gluconeogenic substrate** — one of the raw materials for making glucose you met in Chapter 6.
- **Butyrate** (the *least* abundant) is the star locally: it is the **preferred fuel of the
  colonocytes**, supplying the bulk of the colon lining's energy [@roediger1982colonocyte;
  @donohoe2011butyrate].

::: {.callout-important}
## Abundance is not importance
It is tempting to assume butyrate must be the most abundant SCFA because it is the most talked-about.
The opposite is true — butyrate is the *least* abundant of the three, yet it is the one the colon
lining runs on. (And note: oral **butyrate supplements** are poorly absorbed and mostly reach the
bloodstream, not the colon — so most "butyrate pills cure X" claims are marketing ahead of evidence.)
:::

The three are strikingly simple molecules — chains of just two, three and four carbons. Rotate them
below; the only difference between the systemic fuel, the liver's substrate and the colonocyte's own
fuel is a carbon or two of chain length.

<div data-island="molecule" data-src="/structures/scfa.mol.yml"></div>

**Fermentation also makes gas — and this is the bloating you met in Chapter 5.** Alongside the
SCFAs, fermentation releases **hydrogen (H₂), carbon dioxide (CO₂)** and, in some people,
**methane (CH₄)**. When a lot of fermentable substrate arrives at once — undigested lactose (the
Chapter 5 hand-off), beans, onions, or certain FODMAPs — more gas is made, and the distension is
felt as bloating and wind. Some people carry **methanogenic archaea** (such as *Methanobrevibacter
smithii*) that consume H₂ to make CH₄, keeping hydrogen low so fermentation can keep running
[@chaudhary2018methanogens]. Gas is usually a sign the system is *working*, not failing.

**Why diversity matters — microbes feed each other.** A diverse microbiome is a resilient one
because microbes **cross-feed**: many butyrate producers cannot break fibre down themselves. Primary
degraders (like *Bifidobacterium*) ferment fibre into intermediates — lactate and acetate — which
butyrate producers (*Faecalibacterium prausnitzii*, *Roseburia*) then convert to butyrate. **One
microbe's waste is another's food**, so a *diverse* menu of fibres feeds a *diverse* community
[@riviere2016crossfeeding].

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-crossfeeding
%%| fig-cap: "Cross-feeding: many butyrate producers can't break fibre down themselves — they live on what the primary degraders release. Diversity of fibre feeds diversity of microbes."
flowchart LR
  F["Dietary fibre +<br/>resistant starch"] --> PD["Primary degraders<br/>(e.g. Bifidobacterium)"]
  PD --> INT["Lactate + acetate"]
  INT --> BP["Butyrate producers<br/>(F. prausnitzii, Roseburia)"]
  BP --> BUT["Butyrate<br/>→ fuels the colon lining"]
```

**And it protects the gut wall.** The SCFAs lower colonic pH, which discourages acid-sensitive
pathogens, and butyrate fuels the epithelium and supports the protective **mucus barrier**. The flip
side is a real concern: when **fibre runs short**, the microbiota can start eroding that mucus layer
for nutrients instead — thinning the gut's defences (shown clearly in gnotobiotic mice)
[@desai2016fibre]. The one-line version: **feed your bugs fibre, and they pay you back in
SCFAs and a healthier gut lining.**

::: {.callout-note}
## 🔬 Food science: not all fibre ferments the same way
Fibre is the *substrate* here (its chemistry is Chapter 3's); what matters for the microbiome is how
*fermentable* it is. Rapidly fermented soluble fibres — inulin (onion, garlic), pectin (fruit),
β-glucan (oats) — give a prompt burst of SCFAs and gas. **Resistant starch** — in cooked-and-cooled
rice or pasta, slightly-green bananas, legumes — ferments more slowly, further along the colon, and
is relatively **butyrogenic** (good news, since butyrate is the colonocyte fuel) [@topping2001rs].
Variety of fibre, not just quantity, is the point.
:::

---

## Vitamins made in the gut — and why you can't rely on them {#sec-vitamins}

Your gut microbes **do** synthesise vitamins — **vitamin K2** (menaquinones) and several **B
vitamins** (biotin, folate, riboflavin, even B12). It is tempting to conclude you can rely on your
bugs for these. You mostly cannot, and the reason is **anatomy**: nearly all of this synthesis
happens in the **colon**, *downstream* of where those vitamins are absorbed.

The cleanest example is **vitamin B12**. Most gut microbes actually *consume* B12, and the little
they make is produced past the terminal ileum — the exact site where B12 (with intrinsic factor) is
absorbed (Chapter 5). So **colonic microbial B12 is essentially unavailable to you**, and dietary,
animal-source B12 remains the functional supply [@degnan2014b12]. Bacterial **K2** is likewise poorly
available, and diet stays the principal source of vitamin K [@lai2022vitk]. The vitamins themselves
are Chapter 4's; here the lesson is simply: *"made in the gut" does not mean "available to you."*

---

## Feeding and shaping your microbiome {#sec-feeding}

**Diet is the dominant, fast-acting, *reversible* lever.** Switch a person between an animal-based
and a plant-based diet and their microbial community shifts within a **single day** — but it
**reverts within days** of returning to their usual eating, and their individual "fingerprint"
largely persists [@david2014diet]. So diet is a powerful *ongoing* influence, **not** a one-time
reprogramming — you feed your microbiome at every meal, not once.

**The lever is fibre *diversity*, not one superfood.** People who eat the widest **variety of
plants** tend to have the most diverse microbiomes; the memorable (if unproven) heuristic from
citizen science is **"about 30 different plant types a week"** — a target for *variety*, counting
wholegrains, legumes, nuts, seeds, fruit and vegetables [@mcdonald2018american]. The opposite — a
chronically **low-fibre "Western" diet** — starves the fibre-fermenters and lowers diversity (the
most dramatic, *irreversible-over-generations* version of this is a mouse finding, so hold the
strength of claim, but the direction is real) [@sonnenburg2016diet]. Whole healthy **patterns** such
as the Mediterranean diet associate with more SCFA-producers and less frailty and inflammation
(dietary patterns themselves belong to a later chapter) [@ghosh2020mediterranean].

::: {.callout-tip}
## 🍜 Eating for your gut in Singapore
The lever is *fibre variety*, and Singapore's food gives you easy wins. Swap white rice for
**brown, red or mixed rice** some of the time; choose **wholemeal** over white; add **legumes and
dhal**, **thunder-tea rice** (*lei cha*), tofu, and more vegetables; keep the **skins** on fruit and
potatoes. The point is not one "gut superfood" — it is many different plants across the week. (Local
context: only about **4% of the staples Singaporeans eat are wholegrain**, so for most of us this is
the biggest single lever available — more on that below.)
:::

::: {.callout-tip}
## 🍳 Healthy kitchen: go gradual, and keep the fluids
A sudden jump in fibre gives your microbes more than they can handle at once — the result is gas and
bloating (harmless, but unpleasant). **Increase fibre gradually** over a couple of weeks and **drink
enough water** so the extra fibre works with you. Cooking then **cooling** starchy foods (rice,
potatoes, pasta) forms **resistant starch**, a slow, butyrogenic fibre — a small, free bonus in
things like cold rice or potato salad.
:::

### The "-biotics" family — learn the definitions {#sec-biotics}

This is a field thick with loose marketing language, so the precise consensus definitions (from the
International Scientific Association for Probiotics and Prebiotics, ISAPP) matter:

| Term | Definition (ISAPP) | In plain terms |
|---|---|---|
| **Probiotic** | "Live microorganisms that, when administered in adequate amounts, confer a health benefit" [@hill2014probiotic] | Live, defined, dosed microbes shown to do something |
| **Prebiotic** | "A substrate selectively used by host microorganisms conferring a health benefit" [@gibson2017prebiotic] | Food for your good microbes (mostly fibres) |
| **Synbiotic** | A probiotic + prebiotic combination [@swanson2020synbiotic] | The microbes and their food together |
| **Postbiotic** | A preparation of inanimate microbes and/or their components [@salminen2021postbiotic] | The beneficial *products*, no live bugs |

::: {.callout-important}
## A fermented food is not automatically a probiotic
A **fermented food** is defined by its *process* — "made through desired microbial growth and
enzymatic conversions" — **not** by containing live, defined, dosed microbes at the point you eat it
[@marco2021fermented]. Many fermented foods are **cooked or pasteurised** before eating (tempeh,
bread, most miso once boiled), so they carry few or no live microbes. They are still good foods, and
a small, short Stanford trial even found a high-fermented-food diet raised microbial diversity and
lowered inflammatory markers where a high-fibre diet did not — a hypothesis, not a prescription
[@wastyk2021diet]. But **fermented ≠ probiotic**: only a defined, dosed, live product meets that bar.
:::

---

## When things go wrong: antibiotics, *C. difficile* and FMT {#sec-clinical}

**Antibiotics disturb the ecosystem.** Broad-spectrum antibiotics do not target only the pathogen —
they flatten the wider community, causing **dysbiosis** and often **antibiotic-associated diarrhoea
(AAD)**. Recovery can be incomplete, with some species still missing months later
[@palleja2018recovery]. The dangerous extreme is **_Clostridioides difficile_ infection**, where a
depleted microbiome lets this toxin-producer bloom and cause severe colitis.

**Do probiotics help here? Modestly, and specifically.** Co-administered probiotics give a *modest*,
strain-dependent reduction in AAD and *C. difficile* risk in **higher-risk** settings — but the
evidence is strain- and situation-specific, not a blanket "take probiotics with every antibiotic,"
and there is a real counter-signal that probiotics can even **delay** the native microbiome's
recovery afterwards [@goldenberg2017cdi; @suez2018probiotic]. (Guideline note: the AGA addresses
probiotics for *C. difficile* prevention while on antibiotics; the AAD benefit comes from a Cochrane
review — related but distinct [@agaProbiotics2020].) First-year task: recognise, don't prescribe.

::: {.callout-note}
## FMT — restoring a whole ecosystem
The most striking proof that this really is an *ecosystem* comes from **faecal microbiota transplant
(FMT)**: transferring stool from a healthy donor to restore a depleted community. For **recurrent
_C. difficile_**, it works dramatically — a landmark trial was stopped early because FMT cured about
**81% after one infusion (~94% with a repeat)** against roughly **31%** for the antibiotic alone
[@vannood2013fmt]. There are now two FDA-approved microbiota products (Rebyota, 2022; Vowst, the
first oral one, 2023) [@fda2022rebyota; @fda2023vowst]. **But** FMT is proven only for recurrent
*C. difficile* — **not** for obesity, IBS, autism or "wellness" — and it carries a real transmission
risk (a 2019 safety alert followed a fatal transmission) [@fmt2019safety]. Recognise it and refer;
this is a medical therapy with one narrow indication, not a wellness reset.
:::

## Hype versus evidence {#sec-hype}

The microbiome is where good science and pure marketing sit closest together, so a few myth-busts
are essential clinical hygiene:

- **"A microbiome test kit tells you how healthy your gut is."** For a well person, no. There is no
  agreed "healthy" reference microbiome, the reproducibility is poor (the same stool can give
  different results from different companies), and there is no proven clinical action to take from
  the result. International consensus discourages them without a medical indication — the money is
  better spent on more plants [@porcari2024microbiometest].
- **"A detox or cleanse resets your microbiome."** There is no toxin backlog to flush, and a
  low-fibre juice cleanse actually **under-feeds** your microbes. The only genuine "ecosystem reset"
  in medicine is FMT, for that one narrow indication.
- **"The gut is your second brain — gut serotonin controls your mood."** A metaphor doing the work
  of a mechanism. It is true that the gut makes about **90–95% of the body's serotonin** — but that
  serotonin runs **digestion** (gut motility), and peripheral serotonin **does not cross the
  blood–brain barrier** [@serotoninGutReview]. The claim collapses on mechanism.

::: {.callout-warning}
## The gut–brain axis: real channel, cautious claims
There *is* a genuine gut–brain axis — the microbiome can signal to the brain via the **vagus nerve**,
via **SCFAs**, via the immune system, and via microbial neuroactive metabolites [@cryan2019microbiota].
But the eye-catching results — where transplanting microbes changes behaviour — are largely **mouse**
studies, and "psychobiotic" benefits for mood in *humans* are mostly small or preclinical and not yet
convincingly shown. Hold the channel as real and the mood claims as unproven; the appetite and
behaviour payoff is developed in the chapter on appetite and weight regulation.
:::

## The Singapore picture {#sec-singapore}

**We are under-feeding our microbiomes.** The clearest local hook is the **fibre gap**: only about
**4% of the staples eaten in Singapore are wholegrain**, against a ~30% target, and average fibre
intake sits below the Health Promotion Board's recommendation (around **20 g/day for women, 26 g/day
for men**) [@mohNNS2022]. In the language of this chapter, most of us are **starving our
fibre-fermenters** — which is exactly why the food-first swaps above are the highest-value change
available.

**Fermented foods are woven through our food cultures — and they teach the live-vs-cooked lesson
perfectly.** Singapore's kitchens are full of ferments, and they split neatly along the line that
matters: those eaten with **live cultures** versus those **cooked first**.

| Still has live cultures (eaten raw/unheated) | Cooked → few or no live microbes (still good foods) |
|---|---|
| Kimchi, unpasteurised *achar*, **Yakult / cultured-milk drinks**, kombucha | **Tempeh**, *taucheo* (fermented soybean paste), *belacan*, fish sauce, **miso once boiled** |

The organisms behind them are a nice aside: tempeh uses a mould (*Rhizopus*); *natto* uses
*Bacillus subtilis* and is a notable source of vitamin **K2** (tying back to the vitamin section);
miso and *taucheo* use *koji* mould (*Aspergillus oryzae*); kimchi uses lactic-acid bacteria. One
worth flagging: **_tapai_ (fermented rice/tapioca) contains a few per-cent alcohol** — relevant in a
Muslim-majority region. And to correct a common slip: **_yong tau foo_ is not a fermented food.**

::: {.callout-tip}
## 🍳 Keep the cultures alive
If you want the live cultures, add them **off the heat**: stir **miso** into soup after it stops
boiling, eat *achar* and kimchi unheated, and treat cultured-milk drinks as the live option. If a
ferment is cooked (tempeh, most *taucheo*), enjoy it as the nutritious food it is — just don't count
on it delivering live microbes.
:::

**Yakult is the "modest, not magic" teaching moment.** A cultured-milk drink is a daily habit for
many Singaporean families. Its bacterium (*Lactobacillus casei* Shirota) genuinely survives the trip
through the gut and has some small-trial signals — but European regulators (EFSA) rejected its broad
health claims for want of convincing evidence [@efsa2010lcs; @katokataoka2016lcs]. It is a fine
drink (watch the sugar); it is not a cure. **Marketed benefit is not the same as proven benefit** —
the habit of mind this whole chapter is trying to build.

**There is no single "Singaporean" microbiome.** Large local multi-ethnic studies find that Chinese,
Malay and Indian residents carry **distinct, diet-linked microbial signatures** — a vivid,
close-to-home illustration that *diet and culture*, not nationality, shape the community, and that
inter-individual variation is the rule [@yeo2021sgmultiethnic]. And because significant
**vegetarian and vegan** eating is part of several local communities, the "you cannot rely on your
gut bacteria for B12" point is not abstract here — it is a real, common reason to plan a dietary or
supplemental B12 source (Chapter 4).

## So how do I actually eat for my gut? {#sec-close}

After all the marketing noise, the honest answer is refreshingly simple, and it is not a product.
**Eat a wide variety of plants** — many different wholegrains, legumes, vegetables, fruits, nuts and
seeds across the week — because *diversity of fibre feeds a diverse, resilient microbiome that pays
you back in SCFAs.* Add fermented foods if you enjoy them (and eat some with live cultures). Increase
fibre **gradually, with fluids**. Skip the test kits, the cleanses and the miracle supplements. Your
microbiome is a garden, not a machine: you do not fix it with a part, you feed it, patiently, every
day.

## Check your understanding {#sec-quiz}

<div data-island="quiz" data-src="/quizzes/gut-microbiome.quiz.yml"></div>

## Practise: "I bought a gut-microbiome test kit" {#sec-case}

A well young adult brings in a colourful microbiome-test report and a list of supplements it
recommends. Work through what the result can and cannot tell her — and what actually helps.

::: {.callout-tip}
Progress is saved locally — replay to explore the other paths.
:::

<div data-island="case" data-src="/cases/gut-microbiome-test-kit.case.yml"></div>
